topic: AI model releases GPT Gemini Claude
caption: Hot take: AI model releases like GPT, Gemini, & Claude are overhyped. Industry myth: 'latest model = best results'. Reality: proper tuning & optimization beat flashy new models. What's the biggest mistake devs make when integrating AI models?
image_prompt: Split-image layout on a dark background, left half showing a chaotic system structure with red glow, representing poorly optimized code, and right half showing a clean, layered structure with green/blue glow, representing optimized system architecture, with abstract nodes and terminal-style console outputs

caption: Hot take: AI is transforming jobs faster than companies redesign work. Stop assuming automation replaces roles, it reshapes tasks. What's the real impact on workforce structure?
image_prompt: Split-image layout on a dark background: left half shows a chaotic system structure with red, interconnected nodes and glowing lines, representing inefficient manual tasks; right half displays a clean, optimized structure with green and blue nodes, and smooth, syntax-colored code shapes, illustrating streamlined AI-augmented workflows

caption: Oxford University hacked. A careers platform vulnerability led to a data breach, with 20,000+ records potentially exposed. Over 20,000 Instagram accounts were also stolen in a separate Meta AI support hack.

caption: Hackers hijacked Instagram accounts. Meta's AI chatbot was exploited, allowing attackers to access over 20,000 accounts. caption: A critical vulnerability in Ivanti's Sentry secure mobile gateway solution enables remote attackers to execute code with root privileges, as documented by BleepingComputer (https://www.bleepingcomputer.com/). How are teams mitigating such flaws in their mobile gateways?
image_prompt: Abstract schematic of a wireframe topology with system block diagrams, dark navy background, cyan and white lines, and red alert nodes representing the exploitation of mobile gateway vulnerabilities, no text characters, and no legible labels

caption: ServiceNow's recent security update addressed an endpoint configuration flaw, allowing unauthenticated users to gain unintended access to instances, as documented by The Hacker News (https://thehackernews.com/2026/06/servicenow-flaw-exploited-to-gain.html). The issue, which lacks a CVE identifier, was reportedly exploited to query instance tables. How are you validating instance table access controls to prevent similar unauthorized queries?
image_prompt: Abstract schematic of a wireframe topology with system block diagram elements, featuring dark navy background, cyan and white lines, and red alert nodes to represent the ServiceNow instance access flaw, with no text characters or legible labels, illustrating the unauthorized access to customer instances

caption: Microsoft has patched an actively exploited Exchange Server vulnerability allowing threat actors to execute arbitrary JavaScript code in cross-site scripting attacks, as documented by BleepingComputer (https://www.bleepingcomputer.com/). Attackers are increasingly bypassing weak authentication through phishing and MFA fatigue. How are you securing your Outlook Web Access users from XSS attacks?
image_prompt: Abstract schematic of a wireframe topology representing a system block diagram with dark navy background, cyan and white lines, and red alert nodes, showcasing the flow of cross-site scripting attacks on Exchange Server, with no text characters or legible labels, highlighting the vulnerability in Outlook Web Access

caption: Ian Cruxton, former NCA leader, now CSO at Callsign, brings 35 years of law enforcement experience to combat identity fraud, as documented by Dark Reading (https://www.darkreading.com/author/ian-cruxton). With his expertise in organized crime threats, how will his role shape the company's approach to authentication and authorization?
image_prompt: Abstract schematic of a wireframe topology representing identity verification, with a system block diagram on a dark navy background, featuring cyan and white lines, and red alert nodes, no text characters, and no legible labels, depicting a complex network of authentication and authorization pathways.

caption: CISA now requires federal agencies to patch critical bugs in as little as 3 days due to AI-fueled exploitation, as documented by Wired (https://www.wired.com/story/cisa-ai-vulnerability-directive/), prioritizing vulnerabilities with automated exploit potential and public exposure - how will your team adapt to this accelerated patching timeline?
image_prompt: abstract schematic of a wireframe topology with system block diagram, dark navy background, cyan and white lines, and red alert nodes representing AI-driven vulnerability exploitation, no text characters, and no legible labels, emphasizing the urgency of rapid patching protocols

caption: The Log4Shell vulnerability has shown how LLM jailbreaks can be triggered by exploiting input validation weaknesses, as documented by BleepingComputer (https://www.bleepingcomputer.com/news/security/). This raises concerns about the security of language models, can your team mitigate LLM jailbreaks by implementing robust input validation mechanisms?
image_prompt: abstract schematic of a wireframe topology representing LLM jailbreak vulnerabilities, with a system block diagram featuring dark navy background, cyan and white lines, and red alert nodes indicating potential breach points, no text characters or legible labels

caption: Microsoft has fixed a BitLocker recovery bug on Windows Server 2025, which was causing some devices to boot into recovery mode after installing the April 2026 security update, as documented by BleepingComputer (https://www.bleepingcomputer.com/news/microsoft/microsoft-fixes-bitlocker-recovery-bug-on-windows-server-2025/). The issue was triggered by specific Trusted Platform Module validation settings, including invalid PCR7 configurations. How are you verifying the PCR7 profile in your BitLocker bindings to prevent similar issues?
image_prompt: Abstract schematic of a wireframe topology diagram with dark navy background, cyan and white lines, and red alert nodes representing the BitLocker recovery bug on Windows Server 2025, including system block diagram elements and no text characters or legible labels, focusing on the interaction between the Trusted Platform Module and BitLocker bindings

caption: Indian startups have faced significant data breaches due to vulnerabilities like SQL injection, as documented by The Hacker News (https://thehackernews.com), which can expose sensitive user data. Notably, the 2019 breach at Indian firm JusPay highlighted the importance of input validation. How are you validating user input in your startup's database interactions?
